data.title: VSDA: A Defensive, Low Valuation Portfolio
data.description:
The VictoryShares Dividend Accelerator ETF (VSDA) is a dividend ETF with a focused dividend growth approach. The fund trades at a low valuation and shows a distinct sector mix, leading to a defensive tilt that has become more pronounced compared to the picture the last time I covered this fund in late 2024. This is something that may resonate with investors cautious about the current environment, particularly with interest rates expected to decline further in 2026. The following sections will break down VSDA’s core holdings and compare its valuation, performance, volatility, and tradability with a broader equity index like the Russell 1000 and its peer group of dividend ETFs.

ETF Description & Highlights

VSDA seeks exposure to companies that are expected to increase dividends over time. The fund follows a rules-based approach, based on the Nasdaq Victory Dividend Accelerator Index, which screens companies with higher potential to increase dividends, primarily from a broad universe of mid and large caps, according to a quantitative multi-factor methodology. Companies selected to join the index are weighted through a process designed to maximize its composite dividend growth, subject to maximum thresholds to avoid individual stocks and sector concentration. The index is reconstituted annually and rebalanced quarterly to reflect the latest market conditions.

As of January 19, 2026, VSDA had $230.7 million in assets invested across 75 holdings, with an average market capitalization of $51.7 billion. The portfolio shows a clear tilt toward mid caps, which make up 45.5% of the fund. This is especially true when compared with the Russell 1000 index, the mid- and large-cap benchmark, here represented by the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB). In this index, large and mega caps account for the bulk of the index, while mid caps make up 20.6%. VSDA’s mid-cap tilt has become more pronounced since my previous article covering this fund in November 2024, when mid caps represented 40.5% of total assets. This 5% increase in mid caps was accompanied by 9% growth in small caps and a roughly 13% drop in large and mega caps, marking a clear shift toward the lower end of the market capitalization spectrum.

VSDA’s top ten holdings are Target (3.52%), Franklin Resources (3.44%), Hormel Foods (3.40%), Altria (3.16%), T. Rowe Price (2.98%), Stanley Black & Decker (2.64%), Brown-Forman (2.57%), Chevron (2.42%), PepsiCo (2.40%), and Medtronic (2.27%). Together, these positions make up close to 27% of total assets, with a skew toward consumer staples and financial services, complemented by positions in industrials, energy, and healthcare, typically sectors with more stable cash flows and above-average dividend yields.

The table below presents six dividend ETFs that will serve as the peer group for VSDA in this analysis. The first is the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYM), a heavyweight with over $80 billion under management, which tracks the market cap-weighted FTSE High Dividend Yield Index. Next, the State Street® SPDR® S&P® Dividend ETF (SDY) takes a stricter approach, following the S&P High Yield Dividend Aristocrats Index, where companies must have at least 20 consecutive years of dividend increases. The next four ETFs are dividend growth-oriented, similar to VSDA. The Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG) and iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO) occupy the middle ground between income and growth. VIG tracks the S&P U.S. Dividend Growers Index, applying a market cap weighting process that makes the fund skew toward large and mega caps that have a long dividend growth track record rather than headline yield. DGRO follows the Morningstar U.S. Dividend Growth Index and uses a rules-based selection process focused on sustainable dividend growth and balance-sheet quality. Meanwhile, First Trust Rising Dividend Achievers ETF (RDVY) leans into mid-cap stocks, as the fund tracks the equal-weighted Nasdaq U.S. Rising Dividend Achievers Index. Finally, T. Rowe Price Dividend Growth ETF (TDVG) is an actively managed strategy, blending fundamental research with dividend-growth screens.

VSDA’s largest sector is consumer staples, which accounts for 32.2% of the portfolio, a large position that has increased from 26.0% in November 2024, the last time I covered the fund. This is followed by financial services (20.2%) and industrials (14.6%), forming a core that blends defensive and economically sensitive areas. A second tier includes healthcare (8.0%) and basic materials (7.7%), while smaller allocations are spread across consumer discretionary (5.4%), energy (4.5%), utilities (4.4%), and technology (3.0%).

Given this sector mix, VSDA is heavily overweight in consumer staples (+27.4%), financial services (+7.2%), and industrials (+5.7%). Despite a 4.3% drop in financial services exposure in late 2024, the fund maintains a large overweight position in conservative areas, which is consistent with its dividend approach. At the same time, VSDA underweights technology (-29.9%), communication services (-10.4%), and consumer discretionary (-5.4%), all growth sectors that will leave the fund with a strong defensive tilt, as we can see in the following sections.

Breaking down VSDA’s portfolio, in consumer staples, despite its outsized weight, the fund holds only 15 companies. The biggest slice is in household products through Kimberly-Clark, cleaning and disinfecting brands supplier Clorox, and Procter & Gamble. Other segments include packaged foods & meats via Hormel Foods and McCormick, soft drinks (PepsiCo and Coca-Cola), and tobacco (Altria). The fund also has smaller stakes in grocery chain operator Kroger and foodservice distribution player Sysco.

The second-biggest sector, financials, includes 16 holdings, with an overweight allocation to asset management and custody banks with positions in Franklin Resources, T. Rowe Price, and SEI Investments. The insurance exposure is also significant and spread across names such as Aflac and Old Republic, as well as P&C insurers Travelers, Cincinnati Financial, and Chubb. The fund also holds global payments giants Visa and Mastercard, while Commerce Bancshares and First Citizens Bancshares are among the few holdings in banks.

In industrials, VSDA owns 13 companies with an emphasis on machinery, led by Stanley Black & Decker, Nordson, Cummins, and Dover. The fund also overweights the building products segment, through positions in Owens Corning, Carlisle, and Advanced Drainage Systems. The sector exposure is rounded out with a few holdings in other industries, such as Emerson Electric and Union Pacific in railroads.

Outside these largest sectors, the fund holds significant positions in key industries such as Oil & gas via Chevron and Exxon Mobil, healthcare equipment (Medtronic and Steris), and utilities (Consolidated Edison and Atmos Energy). Meanwhile, auto parts distributor Genuine Parts and McDonald’s are consumer discretionary’s main positions, while Oracle, Microsoft, and Lam Research give VSDA exposure to technology.

VSDA’s portfolio construction contrasts with most peers. While most peers maintain a relatively large exposure to technology, particularly VIG (27.9%), TDVG (27.7%), and RDVY (25.6%), VSDA keeps a marginal 3.0% allocation to the sector. On the other side, VSDA's heavy position in consumer staples (32.2%) is far above the peers’ 9.9% average, given a modest exposure overall, especially RDVY (2.9%) and TDVG (6.4%). As a result, although VSDA has a similar exposure to financial services and industrials compared to peers, at roughly 20% and 14%, respectively, the reality is that sector correlation is weak in the 0.20 to 0.50 range with most peers. The exception is SDY, as both share a common low-technology exposure (7.8%), while keeping over 15% allocated to consumer staples. In summary, despite having similar investment objectives, VSDA and most peers take a different route with regard to the sector allocation, hinting at potential room for diversification within the dividend-focused ETFs.

Above-Average Yield And Dividend Growth

VSDA shows admittedly high dividend growth rates above 15% over the past three and five years, which are much higher than average and the highest within the group. Still, VSDA’s dividend yield of 2.5% sits at the higher end of the category, similar to pure dividend funds VYM and SDY, and well above dividend growth funds, all below 2.0%.

Attractive Valuation

At 15.9x earnings, VSDA trades at a discount of about 26% to the Russell 1000’s 21.6x, reflecting both the fund’s sector and stock mix. Unlike growth-oriented ETFs that lean into the high-multiple technology sector, VSDA maintains a heavy underweight position in the sector, while the fund’s larger sectors, such as financial services and consumer staples, trade below 20x. But the fund’s stock selection also plays an important role, as P/Es across virtually all sectors are much lower than in the Russell 1000, especially consumer discretionary and technology, where discounts are at least 40%. Core contributions to this low valuation are Altria (10.5x P/E), Target (13.7x), and Franklin Resources (9.5x).

VSDA lags on growth versus the Russell 1000. Its historical earnings growth of 4.1% is well below the benchmark’s 8.2%, and sales growth of 4.8% also trails the Russell 1000’s 7.4%. It is not surprising to see this gap, given the fund’s low exposure to growth sectors like technology and consumer discretionary.

Meanwhile, the fund’s profitability is mixed: Its EBITDA margin (25.2%) sits about 10.5 percentage points below the Russell 1000’s 35.7%, reflecting a sector location where consumer staples’ average EBITDA margin of 21% is well below those found in large players in technology like NVIDIA (60.2%) and Microsoft (56.6%), companies that VSDA holds no position. On the other hand, VSDA’s ROE (43.2%) is slightly above the Russell 1000 (42.2%), as high ROE readings in consumer staples more than compensate for the usually high margins in technology. Core contributions come from overweight positions in Clorox, Colgate-Palmolive, and Kimberly-Clark.

VSDA is the cheapest fund among peers, as all funds trade at a 17x-21x range. Other funds with fairly low valuations are SDY (17.2x P/E) and RDVY (17.2x), reflecting over 50% exposure to mid and small caps, while other peers maintain a greater allocation to large caps, not to mention more than 15% allocated to technology. Overall, SDY’s valuation is consistent with its conservative sector mix, as well as a 22% position in small caps, a category that historically trades at a discount to larger companies.

Underperforming The Peer Group

VSDA’s annualized returns of 9.3% over the past three and five years have been well below the Russell 1000, driven by strong underperformance in bullish years. More concerning has been the fund’s modest returns when compared with peers. VSDA has delivered the second-worst performance over the 3-year and 5-year time frames, only outpacing SDY, another conservative fund. Much of these weaker returns has to do with peers’ sector mix more skewed toward growth-oriented sectors like technology and consumer discretionary that have led the stock market over the past years.

The fund’s volatility reflects a defensive profile, as illustrated by its beta of 0.81 (3-year) and 0.85 (5-year), which is in step with the peers' average, though below pure-dividend VYM and SDY, where beta is below 0.80. In addition, VSDA’s upside capture ratio in the 63-79 range is the lowest compared to the peer group, highlighting a stronger tendency to lag the broader market during risk-on environments, which has benn a drag for the fund’s performance over time.

VSDA’s average daily volume of $0.84 million is light compared to peers, as most of which trade well above $40 million a day. This modest volume translates into a wider 0.11% bid-ask spread, versus roughly 0.01%–0.02% for peers like VYM, SDY, and VIG. The fund’s 39% turnover is higher than peers, especially VYM (13%) and VIG (11%), though still far below RDVY’s 69%, signaling a more frequent rebalance, likely driven by its quantitative methodology. This leads to a 0.35% expense ratio that is above the 0.25% peer average and substantially higher than funds like VIG (0.05%) and VYM (0.06%). However, the fund’s tracking error is elevated in the 8% to 10% range, above the peer group average. Finally, its Sharpe ratios (0.30 over 3 years and 0.37 over 5 years) place the fund as the worst option from the risk-adjusted return perspective, impacted by its weaker total returns over the past years.

Final Thoughts

Based on a dividend growth-weighted methodology, VSDA has delivered high dividend growth over the past few years. The fund also shows low P/E multiples, even compared to other dividend peers, a category where valuation is below the broader market. The result is also a conservative profile, with overweight positions in consumer staples and other defensive areas, but a quite limited exposure to technology and other growth areas like consumer discretionary that helps keep the fund leaning into stability rather than growth, despite its high dividend growth track record. With that in mind, there is no question that the fund’s profile resonates well with the current environment where stretched valuation leaves the stock market vulnerable to headlines involving geopolitical issues and further tariff headwinds, as we have seen over the past week.

That said, the opportunity for VSDA lies in a potential rotation toward value, especially if earnings growth disappoints at some point, and in better competitive positioning of the fund’s current yields as interest rates continue to go down throughout the year. Of course, despite that, the fund’s upside is likely to fall short of the projected double-digit earnings growth for the S&P 500 in 2026, as the fund's historical growth has been near 4%, which points to mid- to high-single total return this year, including a 2.5% yield. Still, while VSDA is arguably not the best option from a total return perspective, the fund is well-suited as a complement to a dividend portfolio, considering the compounded effect of the fund’s dividend growth in the long term.

data.title: The Bond Markets
data.description:
“Some of the structural drivers of inflation have also weakened. Trade unions have become less powerful. Loss-making state industries have been privatized. But, perhaps most importantly of all, the social constituency with an interest in positive real returns on bonds has grown. In the developed world a rising share of wealth is held in the form of private pension funds and other savings institutions that are required, or at least expected, to hold a high proportion of their assets in the form of government bonds and other fixed income securities.”

- Niall Ferguson

U.S. blue-chip companies’ borrowing costs relative to Treasuries have fallen to the lowest level this century as investors snap up corporate bonds despite a choppy week for other assets triggered by the Trump administration’s tariff threats and the political gimmickry that is now taking place in America. The cost of borrowing for investment-grade companies stands at 0.73 percentage points above the equivalent on U.S. government debt, according to data from Bank of America (ICE BofA U.S. Corporate Index), which is the lowest credit spread since June 1998.

The robust demand for dollar corporate debt comes despite President Donald Trump’s tariff threats over Greenland this week, which triggered a sharp stock market sell-off before his abrupt reversal on Wednesday. In my opinion, the demand for U.S. investment-grade debt is still very, very strong and we are already near all-time highs. While some US companies briefly delayed debt-raising plans earlier this week, the bond market quickly went back to business as usual. Many regional banks were among those selling bonds this week after releasing quarterly results.

Corporate borrowers have collectively raised more than $172bn from the U.S. investment-grade market so far this year. This is the fastest pace since 2020, according to LSEG (London Stock Exchange Group) data. Record bond sales are underpinned by robust demand for high-quality dollar debt as American interest rates remain elevated compared with post-financial crisis levels, making the overall return attractive for many investors despite wafer-thin spreads. In my view, investors these days are buying the all-in yield, not the spread. The current rates are particularly attractive to insurance companies and pension funds, in my opinion, that need to secure long-term returns. Tighter spreads also reflect some investors’ belief that U.S. government debt is becoming a riskier bet due to the current administration’s erratic policymaking.

Some blue-chip companies’ long record of making money offers investors a sense of comfort at a time of great political uncertainty. We’re now seeing more clients asking for corporate bonds rather than government bonds. Corporate fundamentals are still in a very solid position, whereas on the government side, we’re only seeing more spending.

“The strongest force in the universe is Compound Interest.”

- Albert Einstein

Original Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: After $100 Silver And (Almost) $5K Gold, What's Next?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

It happened, folks. Silver prices blew through $100 an ounce for the first time ever in Friday’s session. Meanwhile, gold got within twenty bucks of another big, round number - $5,000. So... what’s next?

I’ll get to that in a minute. But first, check out the MoneyShow Chart of the Day - or technically, the table of the day. It shows spot prices for both of those metals, plus platinum, palladium, and rhodium, as of Friday afternoon. You can see the rally wasn’t just in silver and gold. Everything was moving!

Spot Precious Metals Prices

(Source: Kitco.com)

What’s driving the action? Well, I’ve shared my reasons for being bullish many times since I first got on board the precious metals train in H2 2018. Several of our expert contributors have done so, too - including Brien Lundin of Gold Newsletter, Omar Ayales of Gold Charts R Us, and Albert Lu of Luma Financial.

It boils down to the fact investors want protection from currency debasement, rising government debt loads resulting from aggressive borrow-and-spend policies, increasing geopolitical uncertainty in multiple corners of the globe, and elevated equity market volatility. Meanwhile, central banks continue to buy gold as a reserve asset - after previously selling it for years.

We’re going almost parabolic in the short term, which increases the risk of a painful correction out of the blue. Traders should keep that in mind. But for long-term investors, the forces driving the move look durable to me. If you’re looking for ways to play it, consider the mining stocks you’ll find in our MoneyShow 2026 Top Picks Report here.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: IBB: Biotech's Hot Streak May Cool
data.description:
Biotech stocks have stormed back to be among the alpha leaders over the past few quarters. The iShares Biotechnology ETF (IBB) fell hard from Q4 2024 to the April 2025 low, sharply lagging the S&P 500 over that stretch. Since springtime last year, however, it has outperformed. Big M&A trends and a speculative risk-appetite among global investors have been key upside catalysts.

I had a hold rating on IBB back in October of 2024. The fund declined from $145 to a $107 low, but has since notched marginal all-time highs, just below the $180 mark. Today, I reiterate a hold rating. With the fund wrapping around previous record highs and a fair valuation, I see balance risks heading into the heart of the Q4 2025 earnings season.

IBB Outperforming XLV, SPY YoY

Strong Biotech M&A Growth Expected in 2026

According to the issuer, IBB offers investors exposure to the biotech industry within the broad US stock market. Targeting no more than a few hundred individual companies, it is a liquid ETF that allows investors to express an industry view without taking much stock-specific risk in the volatile biotech sector. It invests in growth and value stocks across diversified market capitalizations.

After a 21% total return since my previous analysis, IBB’s assets under management has risen from $7.1 billion to $8.7 billion today. The annual expense ratio is moderate at 44 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is low at only 0.22%. That’s about a full percentage point below that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has turned much improved from six months ago, earning the product a solid B+ ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. But IBB is notoriously risky, given its exclusive exposure to the often debt-heavy and deal-dependent niche of the global equity market.

As for liquidity, IBB is highly tradable, thanks to an average daily volume of more than 2 million shares. The 30-day median bid/ask spread is also tight at 3 basis points, per iShares.

For an update on the portfolio, the 2-star, Bronze-rated ETF by Morningstar has mixed exposure across the style box. Interestingly, and perhaps surprisingly, large-cap value commands a notable 24% of the allocation. Just 6% of IBB is composed of ex-US equities, so the nearly 50% weight to SMID blend/growth is materially reliant on bullish trends in the US market.

As it stands, the price-to-earnings ratio is 19x. That's not extraordinarily high, but the long-term EPS growth rate is very low, near 3%. The resulting PEG ratio (right now) is not all that impressive. Hence, my hold rating.

IBB: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Biotech Index: Near Average Long-Term P/E

What makes IBB different from, say, the State Street® SPDR® S&P® Biotech ETF (XBI), is that it is weighted by market cap. So, the largest companies in biotech comprise a high percentage of the fund. In all, the top 10 stocks account for almost half of the product.

That’s key, particularly during earnings season, when names like Vertex (VRTX), Gilead (GILD), and Amgen (AMGN) can swing significantly. Lastly, the dividend has remained constant on an annual basis in recent years.

IBB: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, returns have been muted from January through May. So, this is yet another arrow in the “hold” quiver.

IBB: Neutral Calendar Trends Jan-May

The Technical Take

With shares not exactly a bargain today, IBB’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that shares are right back at the previous all-time high, just below the $180 mark. There’s no doubt that the rally off the April 2025 low has been massive—more than 70%. But take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It has broken its rate of trend, which portends a price consolidation as IBB perhaps wraps around the 2021 peak area.

The long-term 200-day moving average, meanwhile, is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. I see long-term support down at $150, and that’s where the 200dma will soon encroach on. $150 is also near the 38.2% Fibonacci retracement mark of the April 2025 to January 2026 rally. I’m not saying it will hit that level, but if it does, that would be a solid risk/reward buying point.

IBB: Wrap-Around the $180 Level Would Make Sense, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on IBB. The valuation is not all that compelling, while the technicals point to a pause in the strong uptrend.

data.title: Littelfuse - Investor Expectations Become Elevated
data.description:
Shares of Littelfuse (LFUS) have been on fire throughout 2025, with momentum continuing early in 2026. Shares doubled from the tariff-induced lows as organic growth returned in a non-convincing manner after years of stagnation.

This, and an interesting deal, makes me interested in the business, yet demanding valuations make me cautious here. However, the positioning and positive trends warrant continued coverage this year.

Efficient Electrical Energy Transfer

Littelfuse develops smart solutions that enable safe and efficient electrical energy transfer. The business has a long heritage; in fact, it will be in operation for a hundred years next year.

The company has sold over 17 billion components during its existence, with 16,000 employees providing such products to about 100,000 end customers, with the business generating just over $2 billion in annual sales.

These revenues are split across three main segments, with the electronics segment making up just over half of sales. This is complemented by a transportation segment, which makes up nearly a third of sales, complemented by a smaller industrial segment.

These revenues are split pretty evenly between geographic areas, with the US making up over a third of sales, complemented by a roughly equal share between China, Europe, and the rest of the world. The main area of sourcing is Mexico; however, many of these are exempted from tariffs.

A decade ago, this was just a billion-dollar business, as revenues peaked at $2.5 billion in 2022, driven by a post-pandemic recovery, inflation, and supply chain disruptions. Revenues stagnated at $2.2 billion recently, as operating margins have been quite stable over this period of time, although the company has incurred about 10% dilution over this period of time.

A $100 stock in the year 2015 peaked in the low $300s in 2021, and except for a brief dip in the spring of last year, shares have traded in a $200-$300 range recently.

A Base Case

Early last year, the company reported disappointing 2024 results, a year in which revenues were down 7% to $2.19 billion, as GAAP operating profits were more than cut in half to $171 million. This, however, came after a combined $170 million in restructuring & amortization charges.

GAAP earnings were reported at $4.51 per share, with adjusted earnings reported at $8.48 per share. This number is down over three dollars from the year before, as the company operates with a modest net debt load. One of the few bright spots was that fourth-quarter sales were only down just a percent, as prospects for growth came into sight again.

Revenue growth was apparent in the first quarter of 2025, with sales reported at 3%, as second-quarter growth accelerated to 10%, largely due to currency moves, although 6% organic growth was rather impressive as well.

Third-quarter sales rose by a similar 10%, with organic growth reported over 6% as the company delivered on some operating leverage here. Year to date, adjusted earnings are reported at $7.99 per share, up more than one and a half dollars from the year before, with earnings seen around $10.50 per share.

Diving into the segment performance, it is clear that data centers are driving demand in the electronics business, a segment growing third quarter sales by 17%, offsetting flattish sales in transportation revenues and more modest growth in industrial revenues.

The company operates with a flattish net cash position, as 25 million shares outstanding value equity at $7.2 billion at $290 per share. This values the business at around 3 times sales, with shares trading at 27-28 times earnings.

A Substnatl Dal

The day before of the third quarter earnings report being released, Littelfuse announced a $350 million cash deal to acquire Basler Electric Company, a deal valued at $320 million once the net present value of tax synergies are considered.

Basler designs and manufactures innovative electrical control and protection solutions for grid, utility, infrastructure, power generation, and data center applications. The Illinois-based business employs some 700 workers, who generate $125 million in revenues, being valued at 2.8 times sales. EBITDA margins are seen in the high-teens, which suggests $20-$25 million in EBITDA, for multiples in the mid-teens on that front, looking rather fair.

The deal has a pro forma revenue contribution of about 5% and is expected to be accretive to earnings, but this has not been quantified. Assuming a similar 3% depreciation intensity (in relation to sales) and 5% cost of debt, no real accretion is seen if EBITDA comes in at $20 million. The higher end of the $25 million guidance range suggests accretion to be seen up to $0.15 per share, absent of deleveraging and synergies.

With standalone EBITDA seen just surpassing half a billion, pro forma EBITDA will see a small improvement as well, yet with net debt seen around $350 million, I see net leverage ratios at just 0.7 times.

Assuming a similar 4% depreciation intensity (in relation to sales), the deal is very modestly attractive to Littelfuse here, with the deal closing in December already. Frankly, this looks like a very nice deal, with these activities being well positioned.

What Now?

While it is clear that Littelfuse has a reasonable track record and has seen real pockets of strength in 2025, after a few more difficult years, shares are not cheap.

The Basler deal will guarantee revenue growth in 2026, more or less, and likely see some accretion, as leverage remains minimal, leaving more financial room for additional M&A. So in between organic improvements and bolt-on M&A having an impact, the stars are aligned for further growth in 2026, and hopefully some operational leverage as well. That is needed to drive fundamental earnings support.

Further sales growth, but also margin expansion from the current 15% margins, could help in that. Every point in margins could be worth about a dollar on a pretax basis here, so a few points would go a long way in improving appeal.

We likely get an idea about this when the fourth quarter results are released in the upcoming week, but the company unfortunately never provides full-year guidance and only looks out a quarter in time. Hence, I look forward to commentary on Basler, its contribution and exposure to data centers, organic growth, margin gains and remarks about datacenters/AI, and Littelfuse´s role in all this.

However, in between all this, I am erring on the side of caution; shares are far from cheap yet, and expectations have been high, with more heavy work to be done. However, the apparent savvy M&A move invites to continue to watch the company and shares into 2026, before potentially reconsidering this neutral stance.

data.title: From Scrap To Gold: How Copart Built A Global Empire On Total Losses
data.description:
Investment Thesis:

Copart (CPRT) is built on the company's status as a structurally advantaged toll-taker in an industry with permanent tailwinds. While the market currently fixates on near-term administrative cost spikes and capital allocation concerns, the fundamental machine remains one of the highest-quality businesses in the industrial services sector.

The core driver of Copart’s volume is the increasing technological complexity of modern vehicles. ADAS sensors, complex crumple zones, and EV battery costs mean that even minor accidents frequently lead to total loss declarations. As this frequency rises (climbing from 5% in 1980 to over 22% in 2025), Copart’s addressable inventory expands regardless of the number of cars on the road.

With the stock trading at a forward P/E in the mid-20s (a significant discount to its historical perfection premium), the current entry point offers a rare opportunity to own a world-class compounder at a rational price. I rate the stock as a 'Buy'.

How An Overlooked Market Created A Global Capital Machine

Copart, like all businesses, was born to solve a problem. Specifically, one that no one wanted to look at closely: what to do with cars that are no longer worth repairing.

In 1982, the salvage vehicle market was fragmented, local, and inefficient. Auctions were physical, regional, and lacked transparency. Usually, a crashed car had a destination limited to the geographic radius where the accident occurred, which meant less competition, lower prices, and a slow process for insurers, with tied-up capital in unproductive assets.

Copart realized that the true value of a salvaged vehicle was not local but global. A car that makes no sense to repair in the US may be economically viable in another country, serving as a source of parts or being efficiently recycled. The issue wasn't lack of value but lack of access to the right buyers.

By the time the company went public in 1994, this vision was already in motion. Long before 'digital marketplaces' became a buzzword, the company was already building a platform to connect buyers and sellers without the need for physical presence. This redefined how the market functioned.

Why This Market Grows Over Time

The company operates in the vehicle remarketing sector, deeply linked to the insurance industry. Most vehicles on the platform result from accidents, natural disasters, or theft recoveries. In practical terms, when an insurer decides that repairing a vehicle is not economically feasible, the vehicle must be sold.

The interesting fact is that a key structural dynamic is growing in its favor: modern cars are increasingly expensive to repair. The incorporation of advanced technology, electronic systems, and EV batteries means that even moderate damage results in higher repair costs. For insurers, declaring a total loss has become more frequent.

The other good (depending on whose side you're looking) news is that this phenomenon does not depend on economic cycles; it depends on the math of costs. Accidents happen all the time, storms, floods, hail, and extreme weather events too. This gives this business a defensive character, though it's not immune to volume fluctuations.

An Unbiased Reading

The competition is tough, with IAA being the primary competitor, now integrated into a larger group. Scale, auction liquidity, and relationships with insurers create significant barriers to entry, building a wider MOAT around the big players.

For insurers, switching partners is not trivial when the current one maximizes returns and reduces operational friction. Still, there are some risks to Copart. The dependency on insurers is structural, covering more than 80% of Copart's auctions. Regulatory changes could also affect margins, especially internationally.

Where Value is Truly Created

The heart of the business is intermediation. In most markets, Copart acts as an agent, not the owner of the vehicle. This means the car remains property of the insurer until the moment of sale, being Copart's responsibility to handle the entire E2E process (pickup, storage, documentation, photography, listing, auction, collection and settlement). Therefore, the company charges fees to both sellers and buyers, and usually those fees do not depend on the vehicle's cost. The economic incentive is clear when inventory risk is minimal: maximize the final auction price.

Talking about the auction, the magic happens in a proprietary platform known as VB3, allowing full digital auctions with buyers all over the world. Yes. A vehicle in Copart's 'yard' can be auctioned in real-time to thousands of qualified buyers in dozens of countries. In specific markets such as UK and Europe, Copart's also acts as principal, buying directly from insurers and reselling in the market. That new risk is tightly controlled since international markets represent a small portion of Copart's revenues (less than 20%, US revenue is more than 80%).

Copart's operational discipline is visible everywhere you look, with virtually no debt, high liquidity, and low intangibles marking a very healthy balance sheet. Revenue, EBITDA, and profits have grown close to 15% CAGR since 2011, with FCF following the same trend (although slowing down the last 3 years).

ROIC remained above 20% while ROE and ROA stand at levels above 15% a 10%, all signs - in my opinion - of excellence. That being said...

Why is the stock 35% off its 2025 Highs?

I believe 2 factors can explain the current context:

  1. Administrative Costs: These ones have spiraled over the last two years. After a steady trend since 2011, there was a massive jump in 2024. That year the market smelled 'something' was broken, and 2025 confirmed that it wasn't just the smell.
  2. Capital Allocation: Copart holds nearly $5B in cash and investments that it has chosen not to return to shareholders. To add fuel to the fire, some insiders have been selling significant portions whenever the stock reached above $40, with very little counter buying.

Those $5B can easily buy back 120M shares or 12% of the float. The market wants to see a shareholder return policy, but Copart seems reluctant. Besides those two points, EPS projections are still positive for growth, although revised downwards, and there isn't anything else negative about the company.

Going Once...Going Twice...Sold!

It's difficult to be immune to such a great performer. Those two points above are, in my view, fixable. And with ease. EPS is slowing down from 11% (3YCAGR) to 7% for a couple of years? Maybe.

Does that justify a forward P/E (multiple) drop from 37x to 24x? I think...it does. Not that 24x is cheap for a company growing profits at 8%, just that 37x is way too much to pay for anything growing less than 20%/year.

Looking at EBITDA, growth is projected to be around 5% and 9% this year and next year, respectively. Their EV/EBITDA currently is at 18x, which does look like an opportunity, especially since it has been fluctuating between 16x and 30x (last 5 years). My projection for the next decade is EBITDA growth between 4% and 6%, with multiples staying as is and no buybacks. That translates into an annual 3% RoR, which frankly does not get anyone excited.

When I do the same exercise for FCF, things are slightly different. If Copart can keep up the 12% annual growth it showed the last 3 years, the stock's intrinsic value is around $40. If growth slows down to 9%, $33 should be a fair value to open a position.

What this means is that values between $33 and $40 should offer some protection for long-term investors in case performance drops slightly in the short term. In fact, at current values, Copart seems to offer an attractive valuation point of entry, especially with significant international market expansion potential.

Technicals Demand Time

From a technical analysis point of view, the weekly chart shows that a rebound to 200MA is underway, but a potential rejection is possible. The price is poised for a strong recovery but lacks volume.

With the company's earnings a couple of weeks ahead, it might be a risky move to open a position without confirmation that the downtrend is over (a double bottom would do the trick for me).

Challenges to the Thesis

No investment is without friction, and Copart's valuation reflects specific anxieties. If the costs of doing business (G&A) increases prove to be permanent, either due to labor inflation or regulatory compliance, the margins may compress.

The ROE drag caused by the current cash pile (and no insider buys) may prevent institutional money from coming in, especially with no signs of future changes.

Also, there's the AV paradox, although this one is restricted to America alone. If level 4 (or 5) autonomous driving achieves global adoption, the frequency of collisions could drop precipitously. While this seems decades away, a significant reduction in accident rates would shrink Copart's revenues.

Conclusion

Copart is the perfect example of a 'boring but beautiful' business. By capitalizing on the increasing complexity of modern cars, this is a resilient, recession-resistant cash machine. However, the recent 35% drawdown is a reality check for investors - price matters, even if it's a wonderful business model. While the company sits on a $5B cash pile, the market is no longer giving them a free pass; it wants (demands?) to see that cash being used for buybacks or strategic growth in international markets.

From a fundamental perspective, the MOAT is as wide as ever. From a valuation perspective, the froth seems to have been blown off. With the stock trading closer to its historical median multiples and an intrinsic value sitting between $33 and $40, the risk-reward profile has, in my opinion, become attractive after a very long time.

The company doesn't need to reinvent the wheel. If the upcoming earnings call provides a clear roadmap for cost control or a hint of shareholder returns, the stock price can quickly pick up new highs.

data.title: Peyto: Cost Goal Met
data.description:
Peyto (PEYUF) mentioned in the latest monthly presidential letter that the cost goal for the fiscal year was met. This includes the costs (variances) that allowed the Bluesky interval "to shine" as mentioned in the last article. This is important because the hedging program virtually assures a selling price. Now the last article did mention that when AECO prices went negative, the company did shut in some production to purchase natural gas at a negative price while sending that same gas to market to make a few extra dollars of profit. But generally, events like that do not last long enough to make a material difference to the annual picture. However, such actions combined with the announcement of meeting the cost goal point to a very above-average management that often makes the investment idea safer than it might appear at first glance.

I have often stated that good management outperforms when least expected. In fact, good management generally outperforms, period. It is just that at some times it is much more visible to investors than at other times. Last year, this company "sailed through" a year that many natural gas competitors struggled with. This fiscal year (2025), it was negative AECO prices that the company moved fast to take advantage of.

I do not know how many times I have seen remarks about how hedging fixes the sales price. This management just proved that to be an incorrect statement. What happened instead is the company purchased natural gas at negative prices while settling the hedges at whatever price existed at the time of settlement. The difference between the settlement price and the selling price was an additional profit for the company.

In this case, the settlement price somewhat limited the extra profits. But it certainly did not eliminate those profits. Another thing to consider is that many companies consider themselves fully hedged at roughly 80% of the expected production. So, there is a good possibility that in management's eyes, the production shut-in was from the unhedged portion of the production. It all depends upon what the final accounting for the period shows.

In any event, fiscal year 2025 is yet another year in which management will substantially (if not totally) reach the goals it set out for investors at the start of the fiscal year.

(Note: Peyto is a Canadian producer that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

Management again has a goal to decrease the costs that it can control. There has long been a motto here to "sell it for more than it costs to produce". Now that has often meant a robust hedging program to allow that motto to work during times of weak commodity prices.

But it also means taking advantage of whatever the market happens to allow for the company (like buying natural gas at negative prices (see the previous monthly presidential letters for more details on this)). As can be seen from the above slide, this management keeps that sales price at a level that just about "guarantees" a profit (and usually a decent profit).

If I remember correctly, the only year that this company ever reported a loss was fiscal year 2020. That was due to the first impairment charge that the company took. Impairment charges are not common here, as this company believes in making money under some of the most challenging circumstances that the industry happens to present. Generally, there is no impairment of assets if the company is profitable no matter what happens with the rest of the industry.

But that makes this that rare company where the hedging program will show a long-term profit even if there are some loss years along the way. Otherwise, that sales price would be much lower, even considering there are some liquids produced by the company.

The other thing to note about Peyto is that it is that rare Canadian company that actually takes off the Christmas (and probably New Year's) holidays. A lot of Canadian competitors have very busy fourth and first quarters (due to spring breakup in the second quarter). This happens because the company drills and completes wells in time for the strong pricing that exists for the winter heating season. By the time December and January roll around, that season is well underway. Therefore, the pressure to drill and complete wells is much less.

Instead, this company drills more higher-risk wells that extend boundaries or tries out new intervals at the beginning of the new fiscal year. It saves the "safe" part of the program for that important heating season in the second half of the year.

Therefore, as the president's letter notes, the company often has results that vary from the fiscal year goals in the first half of the year. But the second half of the fiscal year often brings any variance back to the overall annual goals shown above in the slide.

The first half is important to the company as it often brings more acreage into the "sure thing" category that is so important for the second half of the fiscal year. It also uncovers new intervals that may be a viable business in the future.

Summary

Peyto largely met its per-unit goals while having record production to exit the fiscal year. The first and second quarters will likely see production decline, as is normally the case for this company (unless there is an unusually successful exploration program). Then, as usual, there will be a second half emphasis on having new production ready for the strong prices that happen during the winter heating season.

In the meantime, this strong buy idea will take advantage of opportunities like buying natural gas at negative prices (as happened during the third quarter for a couple of weeks). It also has the ability to somewhat shift natural gas to stronger markets to possibly pocket a few more dollars than the hedging program would indicate.

What the market and lenders do not consider is that these one-time events keep arising so that the company has a very comfortable financial picture that would otherwise not be the case just considering the ongoing business. But those one-time events generate money that can be used to grow the business or increase returns to shareholders.

So, in the long run, shareholders will do better with a company like this even if Mr. Market does not consider one-time events in the immediate current environment. The money generated by those "extra" chances becomes something permanent, either as a shareholder return or as a larger business in the future. It is one reason that this company generally outperforms the industry.

Risks

As the current management "ages out" the incoming management will have some very "large shoes" to fill. Whether the current outperformance continues is anyone's guess. It is a serious risk as new management begins to take over the company. Don Gray, the Chairman, and a founder, for example, is not going to be there to supervise the operations forever.

The past profits of the hedging program are no assurance that the profitability of the hedging program will continue.

Even with the current management, there is no assurance that management will or can take advantage of future one-time profitable situations that this industry is known for (to appear periodically).

Replacing a management like this when there is a loss of services can prove to be especially challenging. There is a very good chance that a loss of services would cause the company's future to be set back.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Franklin Street Properties: Abysmal Stock Performance But Major Opportunity For The Brave
data.description:
Franklin Street Properties (FSP) is a REIT focused on office properties in the U.S. that is currently trading much lower than its book value. Since our first article almost two years ago, FSP has strongly underperformed the market, returning -50%, while the S&P 500 returned 58% in the same time. After already quadrupling down on the company over the last years, shortly following our last strong buy article, the company has lost another 40% of its value. While the company has unfortunately proved to be one of our worst investment ideas, we remain confident about the value of the company's underlying assets - even as FSP continues to struggle. In this article, we provide an update on the company's current struggles, even as the market has openly capitulated.

FSP News

In October the company reported muted Q3 results, skipping the conference again. With leasing virtually flat sequentially, a complete absence of further property disposals, and a general lack of tangible updates, there isn't much to talk about in this release except the fact that the company hasn't visibly progressed since starting the strategic review. Regarding the strategic review, now announced 8 months ago, the company's Chairman and CEO, George Carter, had the following to say:

Our Board of Directors continues to work with our financial advisor, BofA Securities, in connection with a review of strategic alternatives in order to explore ways to maximize shareholder value.

Looking past the corporate speak and into reality, however, the review, so far, has yielded nothing except a steep decline in share price. The "ways to maximize shareholder value" the CEO continues to talk about are elusive, and after 8 months of steep decline, sound rather cynical.

On 15th October 2025, the company filed an 8-K with the SEC, announcing the resignation of Bruce Schanzer, one of the original activists starting the campaign in 2024. The date of Schanzer's resignation coincides with the date FSP stock started to strongly decline (as can be seen in the chart above).

As the resignation itself, however, was only announced days later, the sell-off could not have been a reaction to this announcement. Even though this remains speculative, it's hard to believe that there wasn't some connection between Schanzer's resignation and the stock price dump on the same date.

Since announcing the review, the share has lost almost 50% of its value, compared to a return of over 16% for the S&P 500. In light of the destruction of around $100 million in shareholder value within months, the company issued a press release on November 21st, with Chairman and CEO George Carter stating:

Regarding the recent decline in our share price, we know of no specific reason for the decline. We continue the process we first announced in May 2025 to review strategic alternatives in order to explore ways to maximize shareholder value. That process continues and, specifically, FSP is currently in active negotiations with a potential lender to refinance all of its existing indebtedness. We look forward to updating the market when the strategic process is complete.

As the company felt it was appropriate to issue this press release during trading hours, it caused a short-lived recovery in share price, with the shares jumping over 15%, just to return to a path of decline a couple of days later. While you can contemplate their motivation to issue this press release, the release itself doesn't contain any tangible new information. As of writing, there has been no additional information made public by the company.

US Office Market

While FSP has made no visible progress on either leasing or asset sales, the office market as a whole showed strength in 2025. As office demand stabilizes in many markets and space under construction is virtually nonexistent, occupancy has stopped increasing and even started falling in some markets.

In addition to stabilization in the leasing market, transactions in the office market have shown even stronger growth. As per Avison Young, total investment sales in the US office market grew 13% from 2024 to $41.6 billion in 2025. While trophy assets in growing cities make up a larger share of transactions, transaction volume across all segments has shown growth. Heading into 2026, transactions continue to show strong momentum, expected to post another strong year of double-digit growth.

Financials

Latest financials are reported inside the company's latest supplemental release. As of Q3 2025, the company has just over $31m in cash and around $249m in debt, leaving a net debt of $218m by Q3. While FFO YTD 2025 has been over $7.5m, the company is still burning cash, as tenant improvements, CapEx, and leasing costs eat up all FFO and then some. YTD 2025 AFFO is -$4.4m, meaning that operations are cash-burning even when ignoring interest and some other costs.

Adding further CapEx, SG&A, and interest costs to the company's AFFO, as it stands, the company is set to burn roughly $20 million in cash every year. Due to the ongoing cash burn and debt situation, the company may need to refinance their debt and/or sell assets to cover their debt. As the maturities are just 2 months away, in case of bankruptcy, the payout to shareholders could be higher than the current share price.

Valuation

As transaction volumes in the office market show great recovery, we believe the environment now is even better for FSP to potentially sell assets at favorable prices. We believe the lack of transactions in FSP's portfolio is not due to external reasons but mostly stems from the company's inability or unwillingness to be more aggressive regarding asset disposals. The market is evidently much more active than in 2023 and 2024, years when FSP was able to sell hundreds of millions in assets already.

Assuming a conservative Net Operating Income (NOI) of $44m and an estimated net debt of $220m for FSP's portfolio, we'll use a total capitalization rate for the company's portfolio. With cap rates being reported at an average of 7%-8% for office markets currently, we have a rough range of a market value for the company's properties.

As per the chart, even at an extremely conservative cap rate of 12%, the market value of FSP's portfolio translates to $1.40 per share, an upside of almost 60% to current prices. Even factoring in FSP's struggling buildings and weak circumstances, we believe a cap rate of 10%-11% is still conservative for our valuation. Based on this, we continue to value FSP's assets at around $2 per share, at an upside of over 100% to current prices.

This would imply a total property price of under $90 per square foot, much lower than their average disposal at over $200 per square foot. All in all, our valuation still is rather conservative, even as it sits at over 100% from current prices.

The valuation of $2 per share reflects a discount to our prior valuation at $3 per share. We do feel the need, however, to adjust for the lack of property disposal and put a discount on the company's current management.

The bottom line remains that FSP has an extremely favorable risk-reward, especially in light of the bottoming of the office market and given the potentially lower entry point. At current prices there is even stronger downside protection and higher upside for investors. The biggest question is how long it will take to realize this value.

Trading Volume

As the stock is now trading deeply in penny-stock territory, we'll comment shortly on the trading volume. As per Seeking Alpha, the company's 3-month average trading volume currently sits just under 300,000 shares a day. On more active days, however, the stock can reach volumes of over 1 million shares a day. Thus, trading on this name remains relatively active, and the market appears liquid enough to buy/sell larger positions over the course of days.

Outlook

The next few months will be extremely exciting (good or bad) for the company and its shareholders. The company will likely report FY2025 in February and will also need to address its debt coming due in April 2026. We are closely following any information revealed until then, including a possible acquisition, leasing successes, or other asset sales.

As we believe the company's issues are largely stemming from an inadequate management approach, we also hope for potential changes in the company's governance structure. As shareholders have lost hundreds of millions under the current executive team and board of directors, we think there is a chance of a reshuffling in the company's next annual meeting.

We continue to believe that an acquisition of the company or all of its assets remains the best approach for all stakeholders. As the company currently seems unable to liquidate aggressively, fresh management, however, could accelerate the process. We are closely monitoring the upcoming FY2025 release, comments on the strategic review, debt maturities, and asset disposals. In case the company makes it to its 2026 annual meeting, we are also closely monitoring for any potential governance changes.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Short-Term Energy Outlook, January 2026
data.description:
A guest post by D C

The EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO) was published recently. A summary in chart form.

Note the difference between changes in production vs. consumption. For 3 years from 2025 to 2027, the EIA expects average production increases of 1.6 Mb/d each year (4.8 Mb/d total increase over the 3-year period from 2025 to 2027), while consumption of petroleum liquids only increases by 3.6 Mb/d over the same 3-year period (an average increase of 1.2 Mb/d each year). The resulting stock build in petroleum liquids results in falling oil prices from 2025 to 2027.

US crude output is flat in 2026 and falls in 2027 based on the current EIA estimate. WTI oil price annual average in 2026 is $52/b and in 2027 is $50/b.

A large increase in Henry Hub natural gas price is expected in 2027.

The EIA expects WTI spot prices will be lower than the current futures strip by roughly $15/b in 2027.

Liquid fuel stocks build over the 2025 to 2027 period.

Most of the increase in US marketed natural gas output (98.7%) over the 2025 to 2027 period is exported as LNG based on the STEO estimates.

Most of the increase in US electrical generation from 2025 to 2027 comes from increased wind and solar output. Solar share increases from 7% in 2025 to 10% in 2027, and the share of wind power increases from 11% in 2025 to 12% in 2027.

The share of coal power decreases from 17% in 2025 to 15% in 2027, and natural gas power share decreases from 40% in 2025 to 39% in 2027.

Tight oil output starts to fall in 2026, especially in the Permian Basin, which has been the source of most tight oil output increases since 2023.

Note that most of the growth in shale gas output since 2023 has come from the Permian Basin.

Much of the increase in Lower 48 natural gas production from the end of 2025 to the end of 2027 comes from Appalachia and Haynesville. Note that the EIA chart has the forecasted dotted line in mid-2024 rather than late 2025; it should be moved to the right on the chart by 14 or 15 months.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Netflix: Viewing Hours Are The Yellow Flag
data.description:
Why Netflix Is Back in the Spotlight

It looks like Netflix, Inc. (NASDAQ: NFLX) is back in the headlines for both bulls and bears again following its earnings release. On one side, we have a business that closes 2025 with metrics that, if you look at them coldly, are blue chip. But on the other side, we have that the proposed acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery (WDB) for around $82.7B could introduce an extra layer of uncertainty for the market, as it is a deal that carries both execution risk and leverage risk.

As of the time of writing, the company is trading around $83, and NFLX has been experiencing a drop of more than -30% over the last 6M, which leaves a current valuation around 26x 2026 P/E, and a FWD FCF yield of about 2.8%. A valuation that, in my opinion, seems to suggest the market is questioning whether current valuations correctly reflect the size of the transaction and the company’s future balance sheet.

On the positive side, I would say that in 2025 revenues keep moving forward at a double digit pace, up to $45.2B (+16% YoY), with a margin close to 30%, and FCF of $10.1B. On top of that, the company’s guidance for 2026 points to growth in advertising from $1.5B up to $3B, and, in general, another year of double digit sales growth.

But on the other hand, viewing hours barely grew +2% in the second half of 2025 despite the increase in subscribers, which could signal an engagement issue that caps pricing and retention if the catalog does not lift this metric that I consider key.

Strong Execution, but Viewing Hours Are the KPI That Matters

It is time to start breaking down the operating performance after the latest results. And at first glance, my view is that these are good results if we look at execution, but there is a yellow warning light that turns on if we focus on one of the key KPIs for a business like NFLX.

Even though the company crosses the milestone of 325M paid memberships, it is important to look at how that base “extracts” value from the catalog without the business model suffering. And for me, the viewing in the second half of 2025, with +2% YoY growth, leaves me a bit cold after an impeccable income statement.

Netflix nuances this with the breakdown, where original content consumption rises +9% YoY in H2, and Greg Peters comments:

“we recognize that not all viewing is created equal. Some titles spark a level of passion and fandom that delivers outsized impact”

The issue is that part of that growth is offset by the drop in non branded due to lower volume in the content.

In my view, two takeaways can be drawn from all of this. The first, and positive, is that if Netflix manages to have consumption growth come from its own content, its moat is strengthened since it suggests its catalog has value for customers, and this ends up being especially relevant for its advertising business.

On top of that, on the call Peters insists that this monetization gap between the ads plan and the standard plan is narrowing due to improvements in the system used to sell advertising. By this, they mean that by implementing their own “machinery”, they can provide more tools to advertisers and better optimize ad inventory, which translates into better monetization of the same viewing hours, which fits for me with the estimate of doubling advertising revenue to extract more value.

If that is the case, the yellow warning light of low viewing growth that has turned on in my radar could be “switched off”.

And as for the second takeaway, less positive in my view, is that if we have non branded carrying less weight, the dependence on branded will have to carry more in the mix. And here is where I have doubts with phrases I have read like “engagement healthy”, because I see it as a chain. If there are strong releases like what happened this last quarter, original content works, but hours do not rise, for me the new is offsetting declines in the other part, which does not accelerate consumption, and if consumption does not accelerate, there is less room to raise prices.

This does not invalidate the thesis, but it does change what needs to be watched in 2026. If consumption does not rise more clearly, pricing and retention become even more dependent on catalog quality, and that is the kind of fragility the market tends to punish when the multiple gives you no margin of error. Netflix states on the call that churn improved YoY and that its “primary quality metric” hit an all time high, but it does not provide specific figures in these documents to measure how much of that is material and how much is narrative.

Despite that, the company says churn has improved YoY, and that its “primary quality metric” has hit an all time high, but for me, and despite the fact the company is executing well and everything seems to indicate there is runway, there may be signs of maturity that are worth not overlooking.

Valuation Setup: 2026 EPS Math and a 25x Multiple With Little Room

To start the valuation, I anchor on the 2026 revenue guidance between $50.7B and $51.7B, with an operating margin of 31.5%. Now, to estimate a net margin, I am going to use the 2025 gap, meaning that if we had an operating margin of 29.5% and a net margin of 24.3% ($11B over $45.2B), the gap between both sits around 5.2 points. If it stays stable, we are talking about a net margin of 26.3%, which would give us 2026 net income of $13.5B.

Now what I do is convert that into EPS, and for that I use the 4.34B shares outstanding, so for 2026, we have EPS of $3.11.

From here, I project the following years taking into account a soft deceleration, as the guidance itself indicates (they go from 16% to 12%-14%), and even more so given the signals that they expect viewing hours not to grow much, and that everything goes through monetizing that base of hours better. So to be conservative, I will apply 12%. And for margins, given that expansion is expected, I will also estimate a moderate expansion of 0.5 point each year, which seems reasonable to me given the recent weight and performance advertising is having, so my estimate would look as follows:

2026

2027

2028

Revenues

$51.2B

$57.3B

$64.2B

Operating margin

31.5%

32.0%

32.5%

Net margin

26.3%

26.8%

27.3%

Net income

$13.5B

$15.4B

$17.6B

EPS

$3.11

$3.55

$4.05

With this EPS estimate, my next step is to decide a multiple that can make sense, and for that, comparing some of its peers helps me. Right now, NFLX is around 27x P/E FWD , Disney (DIS) would be in a middle zone, and Comcast (CMCSA) in a lower zone. Obviously, that differential exists because NFLX has higher growth and margins, so it is not surprising it trades at a premium.

That said, a conservative 25x multiple is a comfortable working multiple for me, and the reason I base it on is that the Warner component, whether we like it or not, adds a risk component that has to be reflected in some compression in the multiple.

2026

2027

2028

EPS

$3.11

$3.55

$4.05

P/E

25x

25x

25x

Target Price

$77.75

$88.75

$101.25

Now what I am going to do is a cross check using P/FCF, and I start with 2025 OCF of $10.1B which, minus CapEx, produced FCF of $9.5B, and with 2026 guidance of $11B, so I take as a baseline that FCF will grow in line with revenues, so from here we have $12.3B in 2027 and $13.7B in 2028. At current levels, 2026 FCF puts the FWD yield around 3%, so I will take that as the reference for my projection.

With that in mind, it would imply an equity value of $367B, $410B, and $457B, and if we divide by the number of shares we get an approximate value per share of $85 in 2026, $95 in 2027, and $105 in 2028.

My final read is that, as can be seen, the valuation looks somewhat tight on fundamentals, at least for me. At these prices, I do not see excessive potential return, especially considering we do not know how NFLX’s structure could look after the Warner acquisition.

What Could Break the Hold Case

That said, there are a couple of scenarios that could move my core Hold hypothesis, and I want to start with what could go wrong. At current levels, I see NFLX trading at a multiple you would pay for a high quality and predictable asset, so any slowdown in paid membership growth or in viewing hours on its catalog could mean a deeper multiple compression.

By that I mean the model would start leaning more on monetization per user, and you could end up with a pricing overshoot that eventually hits customer retention, and that lag would show up quickly in the P&L.

But the positive side is a scenario like the one implied by current guidance, where they are able to extract more value from the same base of hours without creating user friction. If the ad supported plan keeps closing the gap versus the standard plan thanks to their advertising machinery, ads growth materializes without having to increase ad inventory, and if, on top of that, branded content keeps gaining weight in the revenue mix, for me that is a sign the company’s moat is strengthening because it would imply a value proposition that does not depend on third parties.

So under this scenario, there would be reasons both to sustain multiples above 25x and for a potential rerating.

Great Fundamentals, Tight Upside, Watch for Better Entry

To wrap up, and as I have mentioned in the paragraphs above, I lean toward assigning a Hold to Netflix at this current range. The company is closing the year in a way that is not up for debate, with double digit revenue growth, margins above 30%, and also a cash flow profile you would expect from a mature business. But for me, the price already discounts this scenario and I see little margin, with current prices already reflecting several years of execution with no setbacks.

Also because my focus is the product itself. It is true that the paid customer base increased, but in this second half the hours viewed only grew 2% YoY, despite the 9% increase in branded, so part of the growth is going to depend more on monetizing this base of hours efficiently, which demands that the catalog keeps supporting the user base.

For now, I prefer to stay on the sidelines, while keeping a close eye on the name in case it reaches levels that increase the margin of safety.
